Modules
Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Data storage and indexing

HLD learning path

Data storage and indexing

Match storage engines and data models to access patterns, write volume, consistency needs, and query flexibility.

Module progress 0%

0 of 5 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Relational data modeling and indexing

Relational databases remain the default for strongly constrained data, multi-row invariants, and flexible querying with mature operational tooling.

Why this module matters Build one clean mental model before scaling it.

Move through the lessons in order so each trade-off has context before the harder distributed systems decisions show up.

Study target 5 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

High-level design interview prep 5 lessons 0/5 complete Follow in order for best results Back to HLD roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the summary, inspect the diagram, and identify the sharpest trade-off.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
HLD sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Explain relational and NoSQL trade-offs clearly
  • Choose replication, partitioning, and indexing strategies
  • Recognize when one workload needs multiple storage systems

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

0/5 complete
Lesson 1 Next
Relational data modeling and indexing

Relational databases remain the default for strongly constrained data, multi-row invariants, and flexible querying with mature operational tooling.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 2 Open
Replication and failover

Replicas improve read scale, durability, and recovery options, but they introduce lag, failover complexity, and write ownership decisions.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 3 Open
Partitioning, federation, and sharding

Break data into smaller pieces when a single machine or logical schema becomes the bottleneck for size, throughput, or ownership.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 4 Open
The NoSQL landscape

NoSQL systems trade some relational flexibility for scale, availability patterns, schema freedom, or specialized query models.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Lesson 5 Open
Choosing the right storage mix

Most serious systems use more than one storage technology. The skill is choosing a small set whose strengths align with the workload and team.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Relational data modeling and indexing

Open

Relational databases remain the default for strongly constrained data, multi-row invariants, and flexible querying with mature operational tooling.

Why it matters

Many interviews reward candidates who start with a relational model and only move away when scale or access patterns demand it.

What you will cover

Why relational databases remain powerfulModel for access patternsOperational trade-offs

Good interview signal

  • Start with entities, relationships, and invariants.
  • Index the hot read paths explicitly.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Replication and failover

Open

Replicas improve read scale, durability, and recovery options, but they introduce lag, failover complexity, and write ownership decisions.

Why it matters

A lot of interview depth comes from acknowledging what replicas do during steady state, incident response, and recovery.

What you will cover

Master-replica patternsFailover choicesRecovery and repair

Good interview signal

  • Explain the write path and which node is authoritative.
  • Discuss replica lag and user-visible effects.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Partitioning, federation, and sharding

Open

Break data into smaller pieces when a single machine or logical schema becomes the bottleneck for size, throughput, or ownership.

Why it matters

Partitioning strategies are common interview deep dives because they expose trade-offs around hot keys, joins, and operational mobility.

What you will cover

Partition for a reasonChoose stable keys carefullyAccept the operational costs

Good interview signal

  • State why a single store is no longer enough.
  • Choose and justify a partition key.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 4

The NoSQL landscape

Open

NoSQL systems trade some relational flexibility for scale, availability patterns, schema freedom, or specialized query models.

Why it matters

Interview answers improve when you can say not only that NoSQL is scalable, but what kind and at what semantic cost.

What you will cover

Key-value and document storesWide-column and graph storesUse polyglot persistence thoughtfully

Good interview signal

  • Explain which access pattern motivates the chosen store.
  • Name the semantic trade-off, not only the scaling benefit.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 5

Choosing the right storage mix

Open

Most serious systems use more than one storage technology. The skill is choosing a small set whose strengths align with the workload and team.

Why it matters

This lesson ties the previous ones together into a decision process that is easy to explain in interviews.

What you will cover

Start with the invariantThen match the access patternKeep the operational portfolio small

Good interview signal

  • Choose stores by invariant and access pattern.
  • Use object storage and search indexes as distinct tools.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
